title: Lenovo ThinkStation P3 Ultra SFF G2 Review A Bit Bigger and a Bit Better
gist: This first-part review (external hardware only, no performance testing yet) covers the Lenovo ThinkStation P3 Ultra SFF G2, a 3.9L AI mini-PC workstation with Intel Core Ultra 9 285, 64GB DDR5, and an NVIDIA RTX 4000 Ada SFF GPU, whose larger chassis versus the P3 Tiny trades size for PCIe expandability while retaining an oddly limited single 1GbE port and zero HDMI outputs.

insights.claim: The author states that the AI boom has driven a renaissance in mini-PC form-factor workstations, spanning from classic small PCs to highly integrated AI boxes, with a significant increase in vendors targeting the high-performance mini-PC market.
insights.speaker: Ryan Smith
insights.so_what: Frames mini-PC/SFF workstations as a growing beneficiary category of AI infrastructure spend, not just conventional rackmount servers or GPU workstation towers.
insights.quote: If there is something nice to be said about the AI boom over the last few years, it is that it has resulted in a renaissance of sorts for mini-PC form factor workstations.

insights.claim: Established major OEMs with existing mini-PC lineups, particularly Lenovo, are positioned as the biggest beneficiaries of the AI-driven mini-PC boom because they already offer multiple size tiers (e.g., the 1-liter ThinkStation P3 Tiny Gen 2 and the larger P3 Ultra SFF Gen 2).
insights.speaker: Ryan Smith
insights.so_what: Suggests incumbent OEMs with broad existing SFF/mini-PC portfolios have a structural advantage capturing AI workstation demand versus new entrants building form factors from scratch.
insights.quote: perhaps the biggest beneficiary has been Lenovo, which not only offers several system lineups that include mini-PCs, but the company is already offering them in several sizes

themes.theme: Product positioning vs P3 Tiny: expandability tradeoff

insights.claim: The ThinkStation P3 Ultra SFF G2 is a 3.9-liter mini-PC, notably larger than Lenovo's smaller P3 workstations, and Lenovo has used that extra space specifically to fit multiple low-profile PCIe cards, including a dual-slot card, making PCIe upgradability the key differentiator from the P3 Tiny.
insights.speaker: Ryan Smith
insights.so_what: Positions the P3 Ultra SFF as the expandable mid-tier option in Lenovo's mini-PC stack for buyers who need discrete GPU/PCIe capacity that the 1-liter P3 Tiny cannot offer.
insights.locator: Lenovo ThinkStation P3 Ultra SFF Gen 2 External Hardware Overview

insights.claim: The review sample shipped with an Intel Core Ultra 9 285 (8P+16E cores, 5.6GHz), 64GB DDR5-6400 CSO-DIMM memory, a 1TB PCIe Gen5 SSD, and a pre-installed NVIDIA RTX 4000 Ada Generation SFF discrete GPU with 20GB GDDR6, powered by a 330W external PSU.
insights.speaker: Ryan Smith
insights.so_what: Concrete configuration and price-tier reference for comparing this AI mini-workstation against competing SFF/mini-tower AI workstation offerings.

insights.claim: Despite the Intel Core Ultra platform supporting 40Gbps USB4/Thunderbolt 4, Lenovo limited the two front USB-C ports to 20Gbps USB4 speeds, a choice the author attributes to the added cost/complexity of the retimers 40Gbps would require.
insights.speaker: Ryan Smith
insights.so_what: Shows a deliberate cost/engineering tradeoff by Lenovo that caps front-panel I/O bandwidth below the platform's native capability, relevant when comparing against competitors that do implement full 40Gbps Thunderbolt 4.
insights.locator: On the front of the system, we find

insights.claim: The system's rear panel has only a single gigabit Ethernet (1GbE) port with no 2.5GbE or 10GbE option, a design the author calls odd given the chassis clearly has room for a better network controller, and notes this is consistent with Lenovo's identical choice on the P3 Tiny Gen 2.
insights.speaker: Ryan Smith
insights.so_what: A recurring, seemingly deliberate Lenovo cost-cutting/segmentation decision to withhold faster networking even on higher-tier workstation SKUs — a gap competitors with 2.5GbE/10GbE standard could exploit for AI/data-heavy workstation buyers.
insights.quote: Lenovo did not even bother with a 2.5GbE port, let alone a high-end 10GbE port. For a 4L workstation, it is an odd choice on Lenovo’s part (the system is not lacking in space for a better network controller), but it is consistent for the company
insights.locator: To the left of the DisplayPorts

insights.claim: The system has zero HDMI ports anywhere: the integrated Intel Xe-LPG graphics expose three full-size DisplayPort 1.4 outputs and the discrete NVIDIA RTX 4000 Ada SFF card adds four mini-DisplayPort 1.4 outputs, an unusually DisplayPort-only design the author calls rare but on-brand for a workstation product.
insights.speaker: Ryan Smith
insights.so_what: Buyers needing native HDMI (e.g., for consumer displays or certain AV setups) will need adapters; this is a pro-market display-output strategy distinct from consumer mini-PCs that default to HDMI.
insights.quote: Between the NVIDIA card and the integrated GPU, you will not find a single HDMI port on the entire system, which is a rare find in this day and age, but feels on-brand for a workstation system.
insights.locator: Otherwise, Lenovo has cooked up an interesting port configuration

insights.claim: Lenovo does not install fast USB4/Thunderbolt I/O on the rear of the system (four rear ports are all 10Gbps USB-A), but offers an optional Thunderbolt 4 upgrade module that adds a single 40Gbps USB-C/Thunderbolt 4 port in an otherwise empty rear block.
insights.speaker: Ryan Smith
insights.so_what: Faster connectivity is available only as a paid add-on rather than standard, another data point on Lenovo's tiered cost structure for this SKU.
insights.locator: Finally, there is a block of four 10Gbps USB-A ports

insights.claim: The system requires a 330W external PSU with Lenovo's proprietary rectangular power connector rather than USB-PD, because the power draw of the 75W NVIDIA RTX discrete GPU pushes total system power potentially beyond the ~240W ceiling of USB-PD power supplies.
insights.speaker: Ryan Smith
insights.so_what: Explains why AI/GPU-equipped mini-workstations in this class cannot rely on universal USB-C PD charging, a practical deployment/logistics consideration (proprietary power bricks) for fleets of these systems.
insights.quote: With the power requirements of the P3 Ultra SFF potentially exceeding the limits of even a 240W USB-PD power supply, in this case requiring a 330W PSU to handle the extra load from the 75W RTX video card, Lenovo has stuck with their proprietary connector.
